title: Image Force Effects on Tunneling Currents in an STM -- I `Point charge in the Barrier Region' - Model
abstract: In a Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM), when a tunneling electron treated as a point charge enters the barrier region between the tip and the sample, it induces image charges on the conducting surfaces, which modifies the shape of the potential barrier it sees. In this paper, the effect of the modification in the barrier potential due to these induced charges on the tunneling current density and currents in an STM,is studied as a function of the tip-sample distance $d$ and the Bias Potential $eV_b$. The image potential is found to reduce the height and the effective width of the potential barrier, leading to a huge increase in the tunneling current densities. This huge increase (by several order of magnitudes) is however unreasonable, prompting a revisit of the assumption that the electron in the barrier region is a point particle.
summary: This paper examines how image forces affect tunneling currents in an STM. It finds that treating the electron as a point charge leads to an unphysically large increase in predicted current, necessitating a move toward models that treat the electron as a distributed charge density.
main_problem: Investigating how the Self-Image Potential (SIP) modifies the tunneling barrier and current in an STM, and addressing the physically unrealistic increase in current predicted by the point-charge model.
main_result: The inclusion of the SIP term leads to an enormous, unrealistic increase in tunneling current (by 4 to 10 orders of magnitude), suggesting that the assumption of the tunneling electron as a point charge is fundamentally flawed.
method: The authors use the WKB approximation and a multi-slice transfer matrix method with Airy functions to solve the Schrödinger equation, alongside the Saenz-Garcia method for 3D geometries.
model_or_system: A Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) junction consisting of a conducting tip and sample separated by a vacuum barrier, modeled using both planar and prolate spheroidal (confocal hyperboloid) geometries.
key_observables: Tunneling current density (J_Net), tunneling probability, transmission amplitude (T), and the Increase Factor (IF).
important_parameters: Tip-sample distance (d), applied bias potential (eV_b), tip radius of curvature (R), and electrode work functions.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption being critiqued is that the tunneling electron can be treated as a point charge; the model also uses the WKB approximation and a multi-slice linear potential approximation.
figures_summary: Figures show the STM setup, image-charge construction, modified potential barrier shapes, and plots of tunneling current density vs. bias, distance, and tip radius, comparing trapezoidal and SIP-modified models.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the electrostatic setup and point-charge model, progresses to numerical WKB and transfer matrix solutions for the SIP-modified potential, extends the model to 3D geometries, and concludes by critiquing the point-charge assumption.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
